title: Is Trans-Atlanticism Dead? A Debate.
gist: An FP Live event page frames a live debate between Nathalie Tocci (trans-Atlanticism is broken beyond repair after a fundamental breach of trust) and Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer (the relationship persists but is being renegotiated, with Europe's defense-spending surge marking its geopolitical maturation), moderated by FP editor Ravi Agrawal.

insights.claim: Nathalie Tocci argues the trans-Atlantic relationship cannot be rebuilt as it was, because the liberal order that gave trans-Atlanticism its context is gone and a fundamental breach of trust has occurred between the U.S. and Europe under Trump's second term.
insights.speaker: Nathalie Tocci
insights.so_what: A leading EU foreign-policy voice declaring trans-Atlanticism structurally broken, not just strained, is a signal for investors to price a durable rather than cyclical rift in U.S.-Europe coordination on defense, trade, and sanctions.
insights.quote: The liberal order that gave trans-Atlanticism its context is gone, and a fundamental breach of trust has occurred.
insights.locator: Nathalie Tocci argues it cannot be rebuilt as it was

insights.claim: Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer counters that trans-Atlanticism was never based only on shared values but also on security, economic, and technological interests, and that Europe's surge in defense spending signals its long-overdue emergence as a geopolitically mature partner rather than the alliance's demise.
insights.speaker: Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer
insights.so_what: The counter-thesis reframes European defense-spending increases as evidence of alliance renegotiation, not collapse — relevant for gauging whether European defense-sector spending is a durable secular trend or a reaction to a temporary rupture.
insights.quote: Europe’s surge in defense spending signals its long-overdue emergence as a geopolitically mature partner.
insights.locator: Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer sees it differently

insights.claim: Tocci's companion essay, 'The End of Trans-Atlanticism,' argues trans-Atlanticism is finished as an ideology, even though some form of the underlying U.S.-Europe relationship persists.
insights.speaker: Nathalie Tocci
insights.so_what: Distinguishes ideological trans-Atlanticism (dead, per Tocci) from the practical relationship (ongoing) — a useful framing for separating rhetoric from actual policy coordination when assessing U.S.-EU alignment.
insights.quote: As an ideology, it’s over. But the relationship is not.

insights.claim: De Hoop Scheffer's companion essay, 'Trans-Atlanticism Isn't Dead—It's Being Renegotiated,' dismisses claims of NATO's collapse as overstated.
insights.speaker: Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer
insights.so_what: Signals that not all senior transatlantic-security voices accept the 'NATO is dying' narrative — worth weighing against more alarmist commentary when assessing alliance-cohesion risk.
insights.quote: Reports of NATO’s demise have been greatly exaggerated.

insights.claim: The debate, scheduled for July 14, 2026, pits Istituto Affari Internazionali director Nathalie Tocci (a former special advisor to EU High Representatives Mogherini and Borrell) against German Marshall Fund president Alexandra de Hoop Scheffer (a former advisor to the French government), moderated by FP editor in chief Ravi Agrawal.
insights.speaker: Foreign Policy
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific institutional voices (Istituto Affari Internazionali, German Marshall Fund) currently framing elite debate on the future of trans-Atlanticism — useful for tracking where this discourse is being shaped.
